title: The Federal Workforce Was Already Gone — Remote Work Just Proved It

source.original: Tariffs, DOGE & Fixing America: Peter Thiel's Skeptic Solutions

body_html:
For decades, the conventional wisdom held that government only grows and never shrinks. Milton Friedman famously observed that no government program has ever been truly defunded once established. But something unprecedented happened between 2020 and 2025: five years of remote work served as an unintentional audit of the entire federal workforce, and the results were devastating for defenders of the status quo.

The insight is deceptively simple. When 90% of the federal workforce shifted to remote operations, the machinery of government continued to function at roughly the same level — which revealed that much of the in-person work had been performative all along. People who had suspicions about government productivity before 2020 now had overwhelming empirical evidence. The workforce wasn't contributing before, and their absence from offices merely made that fact undeniable.

This creates what might be the most favorable conditions for government reform in a generation. The public, having watched this experiment play out in real time, is psychologically prepared for dramatic cuts in a way that would have been inconceivable in 2019. The usual defense — that every government employee is essential — has been fatally undermined by lived experience.

What makes this moment especially potent is the convergence with AI capabilities. Whether AI actually replaces government functions or merely serves as political cover for eliminating positions that were already hollow, the narrative of technological displacement gives reformers a forward-looking justification rather than a purely austerity-driven one. The question is no longer whether the cuts are possible but whether the political will exists to make them permanent before the window closes.
